sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Emily Hernandez, a 20-year-old female, presents for her yearly follow-up for asthma, allergic rhinitis, and food allergy.
She was switched from symbicort to wixela due to insurance changes.
She uses zyrtec in the spring, summer, and fall for seasonal allergy symptoms, which tend to work well.
She has a known peanut allergy but tolerates almonds and cashews.
She has a history of vaping and smoking, but has been trying to cut back.
Past medical history: Emily has asthma, allergic rhinitis, and a food allergy.
Review of systems: Emily denies any urgent care or ER visits for breathing issues in the past year.
She has not been on prednisone for breathing issues.
Current medications:
Wixela 250/50 dose, taken once in the morning and once at night
Zyrtec, taken as needed for seasonal allergy symptoms
Albuterol inhaler, used rarely for exertional symptoms
Epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen)
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned
Physical exam: The doctor observes a little bit of wax on both sides of the patient's ears.
The patient's lungs sound terrific, and the doctor is unable to find any issues with her heart.
Test Results: The patient's breathing test results are similar to those from 2 years ago, and her numbers are actually a little bit better.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's asthma appears to be well-controlled, and she may not need the combination medication in Wixela.
Problem list: Asthma; Allergic rhinitis; Food allergy (peanuts); Tree nut allergy (almonds and cashews); History of smoking and vaping.
Plan:
I will call in a prescription for Alvesco (80 mcg), to be taken 2 puffs twice a day.
The patient will use her albuterol inhaler as needed for exertional symptoms and continue to take zyrtec as needed for seasonal allergy symptoms.
She will also refill her EpiPen and albuterol inhaler.
Follow-up:
The patient will return in 4 months for a follow-up appointment and repeat spirometry.
